title: Used AI to turn a research paper into a working backtest instead of coding it manually
author: wealthlearn
selftext:
I tried something this week instead of hand-coding a strategy from a research paper. I dropped the paper into an AI layer and let it produce backtest-ready logic, then ran the backtest and looked at the results.
The pain point I keep hitting: most interesting strategies live in PDFs, blog posts, or trader notes. Translating that into clean code, parameters, entry and exit rules, and risk handling is the part that kills momentum. By the time you finish coding it, you have not even validated whether the idea is worth testing.
The workflow I ended up with: strategy idea or research paper goes in, AI turns it into rule logic, the backtest engine returns win rate, profit factor, drawdown, trade count, session and regime breakdowns, then a prop firm simulation layer runs Monte Carlo style eval paths so you can see rough pass odds under things like a daily loss limit and a trailing drawdown. If the rules look real, export to local automation and run them through TopstepX.
What surprised me was how often a strategy looked great in aggregate but collapsed inside a specific regime or session. The session and regime splits killed more ideas than the headline drawdown ever did.
Not claiming AI turns bad ideas into good ones. It does not. It just compresses the loop between interesting paper and actually tested so you can throw away weak ideas faster.
Has anyone here had luck pushing AI past code generation into actually running the validation loop end to end?

comments.author: Dear-Confusion5388
comments.body: The regime/session splits are the part most AI backtest workflows still underweight
